These examples demonstrate that changing from active voice to passive voice does not necessarily change the meaning of the sentence, but rather shifts emphasis from who did something (active) to what was done or by whom (passive). In some cases, it can also make the sentence sound more formal or impersonal. It's important to consider whether this shift is appropriate based on context and style requirements when using either voice. Remember, both voices have their uses and can be effective depending on the situation. Practice helps develop your ability to seamlessly switch between these two forms naturally! 

In summary, the key difference lies in how verbs are used - active involves doing the action directly, while passive focuses on receiving the action without specifying who performed it. Both effectively convey information about actions and outcomes but emphasize different aspects of communication. Whether you use active or passive voice depends on what tone and intent you want to achieve within the text. Engaging with practice exercises will strengthen your skill in switching effortlessly between these styles, enhancing clarity and versatility in writing.
